questions.question: Explain the fundamental difference between a top-level container and a general-purpose container in the Swing hierarchy. Provide an example of each.
keyPoints: Top-level containers are heavyweight and provide the foundation for the GUI, e.g., JFrame, JDialog, JApplet.
They are not placed inside other containers and handle the OS-level window management.
General-purpose containers (e.g., JPanel, JScrollPane) are lightweight components used to organize and manage the layout of other components within a top-level container.

questions.question: Describe the purpose and key benefit of the Model-View-Controller (MVC) architecture as it is applied in many Swing components.
keyPoints: The MVC pattern separates the data (Model) from its visual representation (View) and the logic that handles user input (Controller).
A key benefit is that it allows multiple different views to use the same underlying data model.
This separation makes the code more modular, easier to maintain, and supports pluggable look-and-feel.

questions.question: A complex form requires immediate feedback and validation across multiple interdependent fields. Compare and contrast using a DocumentListener on the text components versus implementing a custom data model, discussing the trade-offs in maintainability, complexity, and performance.
keyPoints: Using DocumentListeners: Simpler to implement initially but can lead to complex, tightly coupled code as validation logic is scattered across individual components. Performance may suffer with complex, chained updates.

Custom Data Model: More complex initial setup but centralizes the data and validation logic, adhering to MVC principles. This improves maintainability, reduces coupling, and can optimize performance by batching updates.
The choice depends on the form's complexity; a custom model is the more robust and scalable solution for advanced scenarios.
